The Corvo Tidal Grid is a fictional renewable-energy project off the coast
of the Azores. It consists of 87 underwater turbines arranged in three
crescent-shaped arrays named Alba, Bruma, and Cinza.

The grid reached full operation on 14 February 2023. At spring tide it
delivers 310 megawatt-hours per day into the island interconnector, roughly
enough for 42,000 homes. Its turbines are rated for a 25-year service life
and are inspected by a crawler robot called "Limpet" every 180 days.

The project's chief engineer is Rosa Andrade Peixoto. Her team of 23
operates from a control room in the village of Ponta do Baleal. The grid's
most unusual feature is a "kelp corridor": a 1.8-kilometer strip of
cultivated kelp planted to dampen turbulence between the Bruma and Cinza
arrays, which raised overall efficiency by 6 percent.
